$comment: The research wiki glossary: community + domain terms a newcomer meets on the wiki, each with a short definition and a `see` link to the page that goes deep. Rendered at /research/glossary. Sorted alphabetically at render time. tier: 1 = E2/community jargon, 2 = loaded CS/CSP term, 3 = notation/measurement. Extend freely; keep definitions to one or two sentences.

terms.def: The global constraint that each of the 256 pieces is used exactly once, filtered completely in polynomial time by a bipartite-matching check that deletes any piece no longer placeable.
Adaptive Large Neighbourhood Search: destroy part of a board, rebuild it better, and learn which demolitions pay. The most reliable board polisher measured here.
Local search that treats mismatches as energy and temperature as tolerance for getting worse, cooling slowly toward a good board. Holds the longest-standing record in its family.

Propagation that makes every cell's candidate list defend itself against its neighbours to a fixed point, going beyond one-move-ahead forward checking. Prunes hard on small boards, fades about two cells out on the full one.
Depth-first search that places pieces one at a time in a fixed or computed order, undoing a placement when it leads to a dead end. Every record engine lives in this family.

Keep the K most promising partial boards alive and grow each by one cell, then truncate back to K. The workhorse behind this project's from-scratch builders; breadth alone stalls in the deep interior.
The outer ring of the board: the four corner and 56 edge pieces, which show a grey (unscored) edge outward. The rest are interior pieces.
How many candidate placements a cell offers on average during search. On Eternity II it stays high deep into the board, which is why the search tree never collapses.
The number of mismatched (broken) edges on a board. A full solution has zero; the community record leaves ten. The cheap score every solver optimises, and a poor guide to how close a board really is.
The community's shared text format for a board, from Jef Bucas's viewer: rows A–P, columns 1–16, each cell a piece id and rotation. The lingua franca every solver reads and writes.
The set of piece-and-rotation options still legal for a cell after propagation. Shrinking it is the whole point of constraint propagation.
One of the 256 squares of the 16×16 board.
One of four optional companion puzzles (two 6×6 and two 12×6) that each revealed one piece placement on the main board when solved.
A fill order that scans most rows horizontally, then sweeps the remaining rows vertically, with the tooth length tuned to a target score. Verhaard's ordering.
Automatically deleting candidates that can no longer lead to a legal board, cascading the effect until nothing more can be removed (a fixed point).
Knuth's data structure for exact cover: doubly linked lists that cover and uncover a column in O(1) by pointer surgery, so backtracking is cheap.
A partial board that provably cannot extend to any complete board. Recognising them early is what pruning is for.
An imbalance on the seam between the border ring and the interior. A completable board needs it to be zero, giving a cheap necessary condition.
The set of values a variable may still take. Here, the piece-and-rotation options left for a cell.
A shared boundary between two adjacent cells. The 16×16 board has 480 interior edges, all of which must match in a full solution.
Deliberately placing a mismatched piece on a depth-gated schedule so the search can pass a barrier it otherwise stalls at. The lever behind Verhaard's 467.
Rewriting the puzzle in another solver's input language, e.g. as SAT clauses or CSP constraints, to borrow a decade of that solver's engineering.
How rich the matching grammar is per cell. The raw 2D entropy is modest, and the all-distinct rule collapses it exponentially, which is why near-solutions are so scarce.
The problem of choosing options so every item is covered exactly once. Eternity II states cleanly as exact cover with colours (XCC), Knuth's Algorithm X being the classic machine for it.
The sequence in which a backtracker visits the 256 cells. Its one free choice, and it moves the search-tree size by orders of magnitude at no runtime cost.
A small local configuration that can never match, so no solution contains it. On Eternity II almost every 2×2 arrangement of pieces is forbidden.
The lightest propagation: when a piece is placed, remove it and any now-incompatible pieces from neighbouring cells' candidate lists.
A border-facing edge, coloured grey and never scored. Interior pieces have none; border and corner pieces show one or two.
A runtime distribution where a few unlucky runs take orders of magnitude longer than the median. It is what makes restarts pay.
A rule that guides search without any correctness guarantee, e.g. visit the most-constrained cell first, or break ties by piece rarity.
A cell whose piece and rotation are given in advance, whether the mandatory centre clue or one revealed by a clue puzzle. Where the hints sit matters more than how many there are.
One of the 196 pieces with no grey edge, which may sit only away from the border.
A property true of every complete solution (parity, border balance, colour counts). Break one and you have a quick impossibility proof.
A board with no single improving move. Record boards are frozen in one: the step to a perfect board is not a local improvement.
Start from a full, imperfect board and improve it by moves (swaps, relocations, destroy-and-repair) guided by the mismatch count.
An edge showing the same colour on both sides. The score is the count of matched edges, out of 480; a mismatch is the opposite.
Enumerate two halves of the board and join them on a shared interface, trading memory for half the exponent. Real on bands, measured to stop paying at full size.
A backtracker that permits a budget of deliberate mismatches, aiming for a high partial score (467, 469, 470) rather than a perfect 480.
The printed glyph standing for each edge colour on the physical tiles. This site can render boards in the real motifs.
Recording that a particular partial state cannot extend, so the search never re-enters it. Pays on small boards; the number of distinct no-goods outruns memory at 16×16.
One state in the search tree. Node cost, the time to evaluate one, is what solver engineering drives down.
Counting something on the board twice, once from each side; the totals must agree, which yields one-pass impossibility proofs.
The difficulty peak where solutions are scarce but do exist. Eternity II's 22 colours place it right at that peak by design.
One of the 256 square tiles, each with four coloured edges. Also called a tile.
Spending a scarce piece in the wrong place early, so a later cell that needed it has nothing to fill it. Where many searches quietly die.
An assignment of a particular piece at a particular rotation to a particular cell.
See constraint propagation: removing candidates forced dead by a placement, cascading to a fixed point.
Discarding a whole branch of the search without exploring it, using a constraint, a bound, or an impossibility proof.
Dropping the whole-piece requirement so a linear solver can place fractions of pieces and reach zero error, then paying for it when integrality is restored.
Running the same solver many times with different random seeds, cutting each off early. Every record solver since 2007 is one.
The proven fact that record boards are locally frozen: freeing and re-optimising the halo around the mismatches finds no improvement. The central barrier of the puzzle.
Boolean satisfiability: is there an assignment satisfying every clause? Complete SAT solvers stall on the full board but still prove sub-boards impossible.
The number of matched edges on a board, out of 480. A perfect solution scores 480; the community record scores 470.
The exponential tree of all placement sequences a backtracker could explore. On Eternity II it is astronomically large and barely shrinkable.
An interlocking loop of piece moves connecting two board configurations. Because every partial application scores worse, you cannot walk between basins gradually.
The craft below the algorithm: lookup tables, cache-sized structs, generated code. It decides whether a node costs 26 cycles or 2,600, and it is why the record engines run at all.
Throughput: how many placements a solver evaluates per second. It changes which boards you reach, not the ceiling you hit.
A board that honours all five official clues: the mandatory centre piece plus the four placements the clue puzzles reveal. Most records honour only the centre.
Run several annealing chains at different temperatures and periodically swap their states, so the search crosses barriers a single chain cannot.
A hash table of already-evaluated states, so identical subtrees are not re-explored. Its hit rate is low on Eternity II.
Up–right–down–left: the order in which a piece's four edge colours are written, and the sense of a clockwise rotation.
A corner colour-pair that no interior piece can present, so a cell demanding it is a dead end before any piece is placed.
A barrier that stops a solver at a score: the rigidity wall (no local improvement), the σ-cycle wall (no basin hop), the depth wall (breadth search stalls). Different methods die on different walls.
Knuth's extension of exact cover with coloured secondary items, which models edge matching faithfully. The clean statement of Eternity II as exact cover.
